retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Infratemporal Fossa -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of the infratemporal fossa is formed by osseous boundaries located inferiorly and medially to the zygomatic arch. The shape of the infratemporal fossa can be loosely described as an inverted square pyramid containing directional boundaries that delineate its area. Anteriorly, the boundary is formed by the posterolateral surface of the maxillary sinus. The ramus of the mandible forms the lateral boundary while the medial pterygoid and tensor veli palatini muscles form the medial border. The medial border of the infratemporal fossa is continuous with the pterygopalatine fossa. The medial pterygoid muscle attachment to the mandible also forms the inferior border. The lateral pterygoid muscle is located anteromedially to the infratemporal fossa. The posterior boundary of the infratemporal fossa is broadly defined but includes the styloid process and the tympanic part of the temporal bone. Since it an anatomical landmark in the head and neck, the infratemporal fossa functions to house and protect a wide variety of nerves and vasculature that course through the space. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Once the mandibular foramen ends at the mental foramen, the inferior alveolar nerve and artery will become the mental nerve and artery. On the outer medial surface of the mandibular foramen, there is a groove called the mylohyoid fossa. This fossa is designed for the mylohyoid nerve (branch of the inferior alveolar nerve) as it travels toward the mylohyoid muscle. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Structure and Function -- Ramus
retrieved_docs.content: The coronoid process is located at the superior aspect of the ramus. Its anterior border is continuous with that of the ramus, and its posterior border creates the anterior boundary of the mandibular notch. The temporalis muscle and masseter insert on its lateral surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical importance of the mandibular foramen comes into play for nerve blocks. The inferior alveolar nerve is commonly a target for blocking during dental procedures. [14] [15] The location the inferior alveolar nerve block is usually before it enters the mandibular foramen. The landmarks for the inferior alveolar nerve block is the pterygomandibular raphe and the coronoid notch. [7] The local anesthetic is usually injected between these two structures to successful numb the sensory territory of the inferior alveolar nerve. In nerve blocking of the inferior alveolar nerve, the clinician should account for the age of the patient due to the location variation of the nerve in adults and children. [4] Also, it is important to account for the possible variations, such as when there are multiple inferior alveolar nerves. Using radiographic imaging, the clinician can confirm the presences of multiple mandibular foramina, since the number of mandibular foramina corresponds to the number of inferior alveolar nerves. This approach ensures a proper and successful nerve block that would anesthetize the entire region innervated by the inferior alveolar nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Location of Mandibular Foramen
retrieved_docs.content: Literature indicates that the position of the mandibular foramen can vary, and it is not always located midway along the anteroposterior dimension of the mandibular ramus. On average, the foramen lies approximately 2.75 mm posterior to the midpoint of the mandibular ramus. The distance between the mandibular foramen and the coronoid notch is estimated to be about 19 mm. The foramen may be positioned either at the level of or below the occlusal plane. The location of the mandibular foramen is relative to the occlusal plane, and it also varies with age. The mandibular foramen is located below the occlusal plane level in adults, whereas it is found at or below the occlusal plane level in children. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Structure and Function -- Ramus
retrieved_docs.content: The internal surface of the ramus is punctured by the mandibular foramen, situated halfway between the anterior and posterior borders of the ramus at the level of the occlusal surfaces of the lower teeth, and through which the inferior alveolar nerve and vessels enter the mandibular canal, ultimately terminating at the mental foramen. [9] The lingula is a small flap of bone that overlies the anterior part of the mandibular foramen, to which the sphenomandibular ligament attaches. [10]  The mylohyoid groove is an inferior continuation from the lingula, through which the mylohyoid nerve and vessels travel after piercing the sphenomandibular ligament. See Image. Jaw Anatomy, Medial Aspect.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Structure and Function -- Body
retrieved_docs.content: The bilateral mental foramina are an important landmark on the external mandibular surface, as it allows access to the mental nerve for a local anesthetic block. This landmark lies halfway between the upper and lower borders of the mandibular body, most commonly on the imaginary line which would run between the first and second premolars, although this varies with age. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular foramen is a bony canal formed within the mandible. This bony canal opens on the inner side of the mandibular ramus. The opening is directly inferior to the mandibular notch and ends at the mental foramen. The foramen follows the shape of the mandible. The canal starts inferior to the mandibular notch and descends until it's slightly superior to the angle of the mandible. At the point superior to the angle of the mandible, the canal will make a turn towards the anterior chin region. After the canal makes the turn, it will travel and end at the mental foramen, which is slightly lateral to the anterior chin region. The mandibular foramen contains a branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V), a branch of the maxillary artery and the inferior alveolar vein. The nerve and blood vessels within the mandibular foramen are the inferior alveolar nerve, artery, and vein. The inferior alveolar nerve is responsible for innervation of the gums, dental sockets, and mandible. The inferior alveolar artery is the dominant blood supply to the mandible, gums, teeth, and nerves in the lower jaw. The inferior alveolar vein is responsible for the venous drainage of the mandible and tissues that are nearby.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular foramen does not contain any muscles. But the inferior alveolar nerve and vessels within the mandibular foramen are associated with muscles in the face and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary artery typically divides into 3 parts. The 1st is the mandibular part, with branches passing through the mandibular foramen, including the inferior alveolar, middle meningeal, accessory meningeal, deep auricular, and anterior tympanic arteries. The inferior alveolar artery supplies the mylohyoid muscle. The 2nd part branches into the anterior and posterior deep temporal branches, which supply the temporalis. The 2nd part also sends branches to the pterygoid, buccinator, and masseter. The 3rd part branches into the infraorbital, sphenopalatine, posterior superior alveolar, descending palatine, pharyngeal, and pterygoid canal arteries. The 3rd part's pterygopalatine branches supply the pterygopalatine fossa. The 3rd part's branches are not critical to facial musculature perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Infratemporal Fossa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The infratemporal fossa is an irregularly shaped cavity in the face that is touted as being one of the most anatomically complex regions in the head and neck. The location of the infratemporal fossa makes it an area of interest for pathology treated by many surgical subspecialties including otolaryngology, neurosurgery, and maxillofacial surgery. The anatomic space defined as the infratemporal fossa contains a variety of nerves, arteries, veins, and muscles. A clinically significant feature of the infratemporal fossa involves potentially life-threatening spread of infection from the infratemporal fossa through the pterygoid plexus to the cavernous sinus. This review article will highlight the important anatomic landmarks and structures contained in and around the infratemporal fossa. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Skull
retrieved_docs.content: The angle of the mandible is where the mandible body meets the ramus, forming a distinct corner or angle. The mandibular notch (sigmoid notch) is a depression between the condylar and coronoid processes on the ramus' superior border. The mandibular foramen on the ramus' internal surface transmits the inferior alveolar vessels and nerves, supplying the lower teeth and the mandible. The mandibular symphysis is where the 2 halves of the mandible fuse anteriorly at the midline. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: neoplasm of occipital bone”, “malignant neoplasm of parietal bone”, “malignant neoplasm of temporal bone (diagnosis)”, and “malignant neoplasm of vomer”, respectively. The overarching category “cancer of bone and connective tissue” is linked by a broader relationship to the Arabic term “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is explicitly mapped to each of the malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid, frontal, occipital, parietal, temporal, and vomer bones. Additionally, “cancer of bone and connective tissue” is mapped to the Arabic phrase “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw), reflecting that bone and connective‑tissue cancers can involve upper‑jaw structures. The classification relationship expressed by the verb “classifies” connects the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) with “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي”, indicating that bone cancer is considered a type of malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships illustrate how metastatic bone malignancies are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy. Cancer of bone and connective tissue is considered equivalent to malignant neoplasm of the temporal bone and is mapped to the Arabic term ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw); the Arabic phrase سرطان العظم (bone cancer) is classified under ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that bone cancer is regarded as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor. Collectively, these cross‑lingual and anatomical mappings illustrate how metastatic skin lesions, facial skin tumors of uncertain behavior, and malignant bone neoplasms of the skull, facial bones, and upper jaw are interrelated within broader categories of metastatic disease, head‑and‑neck oncology, and bone‑and‑connective‑tissue cancers, underscoring the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant neoplasms that arise in the bones of the skull and face constitute a distinct group of head‑and‑neck cancers. They are described by multilingual terms such as “Maligne neoplasma van beenderen van schedel en aangezicht” (Dutch), “nádory hlavy”/“nádory obličeje” (Czech), and “سرطان الرأس والعنق” (Arabic), and are classified both as a subtype of head‑and‑neck cancer and as a form of cancer of bone and connective tissue. These tumors may be primary bone malignancies, secondary (metastatic) lesions, or of uncertain origin, and include site‑specific entities such as malignant neoplasm of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone, and vomer bone. Each metastatic malignant neoplasm to an individual cranial bone (e.g., metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone → malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone) follows the same pathological principles as its primary counterpart. Neoplasms of the facial bones are captured by the term “Neoplasm of bone of face,” which maps to the broader category “cancer of bone and connective tissue” and overlaps with the Arabic concept “ورم العظام.” Regional spread to head‑and‑neck lymph nodes is also regarded as a component of head‑and‑neck cancer, emphasizing the importance of nodal involvement in staging and management. This integrated framework links primary and metastatic bone malignancies of the skull and face to the larger classifications of head‑and‑neck cancer and bone‑and‑connective‑tissue neoplasms, supporting clinicians in recognizing diverse presentations, applying accurate classification, and selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: or discectomy. In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2340
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node and metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are both classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue in the head and neck region. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is directly linked to the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term “السرطان النقيلي,” which denotes metastatic cancer. Recognizing these entities as secondary malignancies and understanding their characteristic metastatic morphology are important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management of patients with head‑and‑neck cancer spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.
